data.title: Delphi Digital: Top 10 Crypto Market Predictions for 2026
data.description:
Source: Delphi Digital, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Perp DEX will become the new Wall Street. Artificial intelligence agents (AI agents) will trade autonomously. Exchanges will become all-encompassing applications. Here are our 10 key predictions.

1. AI agents begin to trade autonomously

The x402 protocol allows any API to be accessed via crypto payments.

When agents need services, they can pay instantly using stablecoins. No shopping cart or subscription is required. ERC-8004 enhances trust by creating a reputation registry for agents, containing their performance history and collateral. Combining these elements creates a self-governing agent economy. Users can commission travel planning. Their agents subcontract to flight search agents, pay for the data via x402, and book tickets on-chain—all without human intervention. 2. Perp DEX Devours Traditional Finance Traditional finance is costly due to its fragmentation. Trading takes place on exchanges, settlement is completed through clearinghouses, and custody is handled by banks. Blockchain integrates all these elements into a single smart contract. Hyperliquid is now building a native lending platform. A perpetual decentralized exchange (Perp DEX) may simultaneously function as a brokerage, exchange, custodian, bank, and clearinghouse. Competitors such as Aster, Lighter, and Paradex are striving to catch up. 3. The Transformation of Prediction Markets into Traditional Financial Infrastructure Thomas Peterffy, Chairman of Interactive Brokers, defines prediction markets as a real-time information layer for portfolios. Interactive Brokers' early demand was primarily focused on weather contracts related to energy, logistics, and insurance risks. 2026 will usher in new market categories: stock event markets including those predicting earnings beats and guidance ranges, macroeconomic data prediction markets such as the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and Federal Reserve decisions, and cross-asset relative value markets. Traders holding tokenized Apple stock can hedge profit risk through simple binary contracts without the need for options trading. Prediction markets will become top-tier derivatives markets. 4. Ecosystems Recovering Revenue from Stablecoin Issuers

Coinbase earned over $900 million in reserve revenue last year just by controlling the distribution of USDC. Blockchains such as Solana, BSC, Arbitrum, Aptos, and Avalanche collect approximately $800 million in fees annually, while their networks store over $30 billion in USDC and USDT. The platforms driving stablecoin adoption earn far less in revenue than they remit to issuers.

This is now changing.

Hyperliquid bid for USDH and has now incorporated half of its reserve yields into its aid fund. Ethena's stablecoin-as-a-service model is being adopted by platforms such as Sui, MegaETH, and Jupiter. Yields that previously flowed passively to existing issuers are now being reclaimed by platforms that create demand. 5. DeFi Breaks the Dilemma of Low-Collateral Lending The total value locked (TVL) of decentralized finance (DeFi) lending protocols is in the billions of dollars, but almost all protocols require over-collateralization. The emergence of Zero Knowledge Transfer Protocol (zkTLS) breaks this limitation. Users can prove their bank balance exceeds a certain amount without revealing account information, transaction records, or identity information. 3Jane utilizes verified Web2 Finance data to provide instant, low-collateral USDC credit lines. The algorithm monitors borrowers in real time and dynamically adjusts interest rates. The same framework can also use past performance as a credit score to assess the creditworthiness of AI agents. Maple, Centrifuge, and USD.AI are working on addressing related issues. By 2026, low-collateral lending will evolve from the pilot phase into infrastructure. 6. Onchain FX Finds a Product-Market Fit

USD stablecoins account for 99.7% of the total supply, but this dominance may have peaked. The traditional foreign exchange market, worth trillions of dollars, is riddled with intermediaries, fragmented settlement channels, and exorbitant fees. Onchain FX breaks this rigid system by eliminating multiple intermediaries by allowing all currencies to exist as tokenized assets on a shared execution layer.

... In emerging market currency pairs where traditional forex trading channels are the most costly and inefficient, product-market fit may be the first thing to emerge. These underserved market channels are precisely where the value proposition of cryptocurrencies is most clearly demonstrated. 7. Gold and Bitcoin Lead Currency Devaluation Trading [Image of gold price surged 60% after we listed it as one of the most noteworthy charts. Despite record high gold prices, central banks have still purchased over 600 tons of gold.] China is one of the most active buyers. The macroeconomic environment supports the continued strength of gold prices. Global central banks are cutting interest rates. Fiscal deficits will continue until 2027. Global broad money supply (M2) is hitting record highs. The Federal Reserve has ended its quantitative easing (QT) policy. Gold typically leads Bitcoin by three to four months. Both assets will benefit from safe-haven demand as currency devaluation becomes a major issue ahead of the 2026 midterm elections. 8. Exchanges Become “All-Round” Applications Coinbase, Robinhood, Binance, and Kraken are no longer simply building exchange platforms; they are committed to building comprehensive, “all-round” applications. Coinbase uses Base as its operating system, the Base App as its user interface, USDC revenue as its trading floor, and Deribit for derivatives trading. Robinhood's Gold membership grew by 77% year-over-year and plays a role in user retention. Binance has reached super-app scale, boasting over 270 million users and $250 billion in payment transactions. **As user acquisition costs decrease, value will accrue to platforms that own users. In 2026, the winners will begin to diverge.** 9. Privacy Infrastructure Meets Demands **Privacy is under pressure. The EU passed the Chat Control Act. Cash transaction limits were set at €10,000. The ECB plans to launch a digital euro with a holding limit of €3,000.** Privacy infrastructure is catching up. Payy is launching a private cryptocurrency card. Seismic provides protocol-level encryption for fintech companies. Keeta supports on-chain KYC without disclosing personal data. Canton Network provides privacy infrastructure support for large financial institutions. **Without private channels, the adoption of stablecoins will face bottlenecks.** 10. Altcoin returns remain fragmented. [Image of a coin: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7426211_watermarknone.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7426211_watermarknone.png) [Image of a coin: fYRYSy9ZwvPE0XxF6bJjtKHdaJeFMFaTqAmM5J40.jpeg](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7426211_watermarknone.png)](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7426211_watermarknone.png) [Image of a coin: fYRYSy9ZwvPE0XxF6bJjtKHdaJeFMFaTqAmM5J40.jpeg ... Ultimately, the winners will be those teams that build a strong competitive advantage in areas with real economic activity. Cryptocurrencies are entering the next phase. The era of institutionalization has arrived. Prediction markets, on-chain lending, agent economies, and stablecoins as infrastructure all represent a true paradigm shift. Cryptocurrencies are becoming the infrastructure layer of global finance. Teams that understand this will dominate the next decade.

data.title: Bitchat Becomes Most Downloaded App In Uganda As Government Blocks Internet To Curb Online Misinformation
data.description:
Bitchat Tops Downloads As Uganda Cuts Internet During Presidential Vote

Ugandans raced to download the encrypted messaging app Bitchat after authorities cut public internet access nationwide ahead of the presidential election.

The Uganda Communications Commission (UCC) confirmed that the shutdown began at 6 p.m. local time on Tuesday and will remain in place throughout the election period.

The blackout affects mobile data, fibre, and other public internet services, a measure officials said is aimed at reducing the spread of false information.

How Bitchat Keeps Messaging Alive Without Internet

Bitchat operates on a Bluetooth mesh network, allowing users to communicate securely even without an internet connection.

Last year, @jack launched #Bitchat, a bluetooth p2p encrypted messaging app. Now, it's leading a revolution in Uganda 🇺🇬

I doubt Dorsey would have thought Bitchat could help Ugandans contest the 38-year presidency (dictatorship) of Yoweri Museveni.

Here's why <1M people are… pic.twitter.com/em97vjwr4I

— Gareth Jenkinson (@gazza_jenks) January 9, 2026

Following the shutdown, the app quickly surged to the top of both the Apple App Store and Google Play charts in Uganda.

Virtual Private Network (VPN) apps also saw high download rates, reflecting strong public demand for access to information during the election.

Source: appfigures

Government Says Misinformation Is The Concern But Critics Disagree

The UCC has defended the internet shutdown as a way to curb misinformation and content that could provoke unrest.

However, critics warn that blocking online access could suppress election-related information and limit public transparency.

This marks the third consecutive presidential election in Uganda where authorities have imposed a nationwide internet blackout, with similar measures implemented in 2016 and 2021.

Official Denials Came Before Shutdown Took Place

Last week, UCC Executive Director Nyombi Thembo had insisted the internet would remain available.

“Why would you use Bitchat when there is internet? Internet will be there, use internet.”

Thembo also claimed the commission had the technical capacity to block Bitchat if necessary.

Despite these statements, the shutdown was implemented, and Bitchat’s downloads have now exceeded 400,000 users in Uganda as of early January.

🔥 JUST IN: Mesh network messaging app "Bitchat" adds ~1M users after Uganda opposition leader warns about nation-wide internet shutdown 😱 pic.twitter.com/5LVAiTD0SY

— Lightning News (@LightningNewsX) January 5, 2026

Election Concerns Drive Adoption

Opposition leader Bobi Wine encouraged supporters to download Bitchat in anticipation of the blackout, highlighting its ability to enable messaging across large groups without internet.

The criminal regime has announced an internet shutdown throughout Uganda, beginning 6:00pm today, ahead of the election on Thursday.

Have you downloaded Bitchat yet? If not, you have a small window to do so. Click on this link: https://t.co/Aog8gOmS2a

Also, have you uploaded… pic.twitter.com/vx9xIJA8wA

— BOBI WINE (@HEBobiwine) January 13, 2026

Searches for the app spiked sharply after his appeal, signalling that fears of restricted connectivity helped fuel adoption.

Bitchat Finds Global Use Beyond Uganda

Bitchat has also been adopted in other countries facing internet restrictions or emergencies.

In September, nearly 50,000 users in Nepal relied on the app during temporary social media bans amid corruption protests.

Last week, we observed a sudden spike in bitchat downloads from Indonesia during nationwide protests.

Today we're seeing an even bigger spike from Nepal during youth protests over government corruption and a social media ban.

Freedom tech is for the people. Please share. pic.twitter.com/IqhRa8eCvw

— calle (@callebtc) September 10, 2025

Madagascar saw similar use three weeks later, while in November, Jamaican residents turned to the app when Hurricane Melissa caused widespread communication outages.

The surge in Bitchat downloads illustrates how users are turning to alternative communication platforms when traditional internet access is unavailable, particularly in politically or environmentally sensitive situations.

data.title: Ukraine Blocks Polymarket, Joins Global Crackdown on Prediction Markets
data.description:
Ukraine Bans Polymarket Following Bets On Russia's Invasion

Ukraine has officially blocked access to the prediction market platform Polymarket, citing its operations as unlicensed gambling under national law. The move places Ukraine among a growing list of countries—including France, Germany, the United Kingdom, Italy, Poland, Thailand, and Australia—that have restricted the platform due to concerns over compliance with gambling regulations.

Latest: Ukraine puts an end to Polymarket operations due to unlicensed gambling accusations. This blockchain-based market, valued at $8 billion, now faces suspension in 34 countries 🌐 #CryptoBanned#BlockchainNewspic.twitter.com/Zg4cUjOCfC

— CoinLaw (@coinlaw_io) January 13, 2026

The decision, issued on Dec. 10, 2025, by the National Commission for the Regulation of Electronic Communications (NCEC) under Resolution No. 695, instructs internet service providers to block websites that “organize, conduct, or facilitate gambling activities without proper licensing.” Polymarket’s main domain (polymarket.com) has been added to Ukraine’s official registry of prohibited online resources, effectively cutting off domestic access, local media reported.

Polymarket is a platform which allows users to bet on real-world events, including elections, policy decisions, and geopolitical developments, with prices reflecting market-implied probabilities rather than fixed betting odds. Despite this innovative design, Ukrainian authorities maintain that such activities still constitute gambling, for which the platform holds no valid local license.

The ban follows heightened criticism over how the platform has facilitated bets on geopolitical events linked to the Russian invasion.

Two suspicious new accounts just bet $23k that Russia and Ukraine will reach a ceasefire before the end of January on Polymarket. The probability for this market currently stands at 4%.

Accounts:
0xfcab5f23db3c53b7df0939deb7ce99adeb56e010… pic.twitter.com/hPC1IPyAO5

— BlockBeats (@BlockBeats_News) January 9, 2026

A global trend of regulatory restrictions

Polymarket, founded in 2020 by Shane Coplan, operates on the Polygon blockchain and settles transactions via USDC stablecoin. Despite attracting millions of users and reaching an estimated $8 billion valuation, the platform remains banned or restricted in at least 33 countries, including Belgium, Iran, Singapore, Iraq, North Korea, Taiwan, and Australia.

Regulators worldwide continue to view event-based smart contracts as a potential form of unlicensed gambling, blurring the lines between financial innovation and entertainment.

Similar measures are emerging in the United States. Representative Ritchie Torres has introduced the Public Integrity in Financial Prediction Markets Act of 2026, which would prohibit federal lawmakers and senior officials from trading contracts tied to political or policy outcomes when possessing nonpublic information.

🚨 The U.S. will BAN Polymarket

Prediction markets exposed insider trading 10x

I analyzed onchain flows, timing patterns and political wallets

Here’s why they’re cracking down and what it really means👇🧵 pic.twitter.com/AAgccaYWGo

— NoName (@WhaleNoName) January 10, 2026

More recently, the Tennessee Sports Wagering Council (SWC) also ordered the ban on three prediction market platforms:Polymarket, Kalshi, and Crypto.com.

Regulatory pressure shapes the future of prediction markets

As more jurisdictions impose restrictions, platforms like Polymarket face mounting legal scrutiny over whether decentralized prediction markets represent legitimate financial innovation or unlicensed gambling.

Ukraine’s ban underscores the broader trend of global authorities asserting regulatory control, emphasizing that even blockchain-based solutions must navigate complex legal landscapes.

The move signals to the industry that cross-border decentralized platforms must increasingly reconcile innovation with regulatory expectations—or risk widespread prohibitions.

data.title: The full text of the CLARITY bill has been released. Will it pass and bring new opportunities?
data.description: On January 13, the U.S. Senate Banking Committee released the full text of the "Clarity Act for Digital Asset Markets," which is expected to be reviewed this week. This 278-page draft abandons the token-by-token classification model and constructs a "channel system" for allocating jurisdiction based on the functional lifecycle of digital assets. Its core is to clarify the regulatory boundaries between the SEC and CFTC. The market is optimistic, with Polymarket users believing the bill has an 80% chance of being signed into law this year. However, senators have only a 48-hour window to propose amendments, making time tight. The core of the bill is to build a regulatory bridge between the SEC and CFTC: "Ancillary assets" that rely on the efforts of the issuers will be regulated by the SEC, requiring issuers to disclose audited financial statements, ownership, token economics, and other detailed information, benchmarking against publicly traded companies; once token control is decentralized, they will be converted into "digital commodities," with the CFTC regulating trading venues and intermediaries. Key terms directly address industry pain points: First, an ETF fast track: after January 1, 2026, tokens that are the main assets of listed ETPs will not be considered auxiliary assets, effectively opening a commodity market channel for mainstream cryptocurrencies such as BTC and ETH; Second, Ethereum staking is clarified: staking rewards are defined as "uncompensated distribution," providing a safe channel for self-staking, self-custody staking, and liquidity staking, while staking on custodial exchanges remains strictly regulated; Third, stablecoin yield demarcation: issuers are prohibited from paying yields for holding stablecoins, but are allowed to earn yields through DeFi lending and other methods, maintaining the economic viability of DeFi yields; Fourthly, DeFi is a safe harbor. Interfaces that are non-custodial, do not control private keys, and do not interfere with transactions are considered pure software, exempting brokers from registration, while centralized entities are subject to strict regulation. Bitwise believes this could drive the market to new highs, but legal experts are racing against time to identify flaws to meet the amendment deadline. Criticism focuses on privacy and decentralization, arguing that universal registration clauses stifle anonymous participation, and government custodians are demanding a substantial ban on self-custody; the wording on stablecoin yields and the addition of a DeFi chapter have also sparked controversy. Although the bill has passed the House of Representatives, Senate negotiations remain focused on differences such as banking priorities, and some industry insiders are pessimistic about its prospects for passage.

data.title: 2026: The Year of the ASI Singularity
data.description:
We are at the singularity! — Elon Musk

At the beginning of 2026, Elon Musk, the world's richest man, made two assertions on the same day:"2026 is the year of the singularity!"

Not only that, in a subsequent three-hour Moonshots podcast conversation, Musk further elaborated on this view.

He said that we are at the singularity, and the development of AI is like a supersonic tsunami that cannot be stopped.

He also boldly predicted that AGI will be realized this year! By 2030, AI intelligence will surpass the total intelligence of all humanity, achieving exponential growth of 10 times its capacity annually. Even more audaciously, Musk believes that humans are merely "biological guides" for digital superintelligence. In an instant, the singularity transformed from a concept in science fiction into a buzzword in the ASI field. Everyone at the forefront of technology seemed to feel a chilling stillness, like holding their breath for the last time before the event horizon. This stillness is not silence, but rather filled with high-frequency vibrations—the resonance of the collapse of the old order and the birth of a new divinity! Standing here, we see the "technological singularity" and the "taste singularity" reflecting each other, and the "intelligence explosion" and the "speed explosion" accelerating their perfect convergence. On January 4th, Yang Jing, founder and CEO of Synced, and Li Jianzhong, Dean of Singularity Intelligence Research Institute and Senior Vice President of CSDN, held a high-level dialogue on the theme of "2026 ASI Singularity Outlook," focusing on the "ASI Singularity." Musk's pronouncement: The Singularity has arrived ahead of schedule. It all began during last year's Christmas holiday. While the world was still immersed in holiday celebrations, David Holz, founder of Midjourney, posted a seemingly innocuous yet profoundly moving statement on social media. He declared that he had written more code in just a few days of vacation than in the previous ten years combined. "Things will never be the same again," David lamented. Musk replied to this tweet with, "We've reached the singularity." Clearly, Musk wasn't the only one shocked by the capabilities of AI. Igor Babuschkin, co-founder of xAI, remarked, "Some years are calm, but some weeks encapsulate decades of change." This statement aptly captures the "time warp" effect as the singularity approaches. Traditional development models are mostly linear, with technological progress being cumulative and gradual; however, at the singularity, progress is recursive and explosive. Even Google's chief engineer, Jaana Dogan, was astonished by the performance of the AI ​​programming tool Claude Code. She publicly shared her personal experience: a distributed intelligent agent orchestration system that Google had struggled to fully develop for a year was completed by Claude Code in just one hour. Jaana even emphasized, "This isn't funny." She simply realized, with a calm demeanor, that things were beginning to spiral out of human control. In the past, programmers were only accustomed to viewing AI as a "completion tool," but now the latest generation of AI tools can not only intelligently continue writing code, but also... The software can autonomously plan tasks, automatically debug errors, and even improve its own toolchain. Microsoft's Vice President of CTO, Sam Schillace, has just astutely pointed out that we have passed the tipping point of "intelligent completion" and entered a stage of AI-driven development. It can be said that AI programming has crossed the "singularity" threshold, and the software development paradigm is undergoing a historic shift. Boris Cherny, former lead engineer of Meta and founder and head of Claude Code, revealed that 100% of his new code in the past month was written by Claude Code itself, totaling 40,000 lines. In other words, Claude Code is writing code for itself! This means that AI tools are beginning to recursively optimize themselves, which is a core characteristic of the singularity. More importantly, the data also supports this exponential leap. According to calculations by METR Research Institute, the length of tasks that current AI models can independently complete is growing exponentially, doubling every seven months. However, they themselves explicitly stated that this accelerated starting in 2024, showing signs of doubling every four months. Currently, the most powerful models, such as Claude Opus 4.5, can independently complete human tasks lasting up to five hours. The "task duration" here can be understood as the time span during which the model can independently complete a task under a certain success rate threshold. A shortened doubling cycle means that growth isn't just a slight increase in speed, but rather a steeper slope on the exponential curve. METR's research indicates that if this trend continues into 2026 (and is actually accelerating), we will see AI agents capable of independently executing complex software engineering tasks that would take humans weeks or even months to complete.

It's not just software development; we're seeing the timescale of scientific research being compressed as well.

Recently, well-known entrepreneur Yuchen Jin publicly stated that if he had had tools like Claude Code and ChatGPT when he was pursuing his doctorate, what would have taken more than five years of doctoral research could probably have been completed in just one year.

Education, research and development, entrepreneurship—AI is redefining the pace of every industry.

Tasting the Singularity: When AI Knows What's Better

Renowned forecaster and co-author of "AI 2027," Eli Lifland, recently released a new model: the AI ​​Futures Model.

This is a quantitative model for predicting AI capabilities. According to the model's predictions, the probability of achieving a leap to ASI by 2026 reaches 25%. A more thought-provoking concept mentioned in the AI ​​Futures model is "Taste Singularity." Taste refers to judging which research directions are valuable and which code is elegant. The so-called "taste singularity" refers to the fact that AI's improvement in taste is faster than its improvement in coding ability. This stage is highly likely to be reached in 2026. When AI can not only write code but also reject low-quality design solutions based on "taste," like a senior architect, its path to ASI will be completely paved. This is because "knowing where to go" is often more important than "going fast." An AI lacking discernment can simply pile on features, but it defaults to an architecture that's good enough as long as it runs. A discerning AI, however, will first question the constraints, then directly reject low-quality solutions: "This design will exponentially increase the cost of fault location; let's switch to this..." Once an AI masters the "taste" of scientific discovery, it can guide itself to find shortcuts to success in an infinite space of hypotheses.

From Singularity to ASI

Looking back at the development of the singularity concept, it has never been a whimsical idea, but rather the result of rigorous logical deduction.

Reviewing the development of the singularity concept, it has never been a whimsical idea, but rather the result of rigorous logical deduction.

Two countdowns: 2027 vs 2035

Regarding the arrival time of ASI, there have been many different opinions in the industry. In the "2025 ASI Frontier Trend Report" released by New Zhiyuan on its 10th anniversary, Yang Jing predicted that 2027 would be a critical point. However, Li Jianzhong offered a more cautious and humanistic assessment: 2035. "The definition of ASI should not merely be a collection of parameters," Li Jianzhong believes, "it should emphasize people's 'sense of gain'." The core of this lies in memory and forgetting. Li Jianzhong emphasizes that humanity's advantage lies in its rich context and memory system: we forget, we compress, we abstract, we discard the unimportant, and we extract the essential into a framework. Yang Jing, however, points out the shortcomings of the large model: catastrophic forgetting. It's like a person with an amazing memory, yet lacking the "wisdom to forget," and even more so, the mechanism to "turn experiences into a long-term self." And this biological "deficiency" is precisely the crack through which human wisdom shines. Looking back at 2025, both guests agreed that this was the year of Agents. A detail in the conversation sent chills down my spine: Over the past six years, the duration for which AI agents could independently complete human tasks had been growing exponentially, roughly doubling every seven months; however, starting in 2024, this doubling cycle suddenly shortened to about four months. Meanwhile, the coding task time that the AI ​​Agent can complete independently is approaching 5 hours. The real impact of this data lies not in the "5 hours" itself, but in the fact that the doubling period has suddenly shortened. Because once the doubling period shortens, the world will switch from "gradual improvement" to "exponential advancement": you're still using linear intuition to understand tomorrow, but tomorrow is already rewriting the day after tomorrow at an exponential rate. Therefore, Li Jianzhong considers Agent intelligence a key milestone for 2025. But even though Google and OpenAI's models have already won gold medals at IMO and IOI, which is astonishing, Yang Jing still asks: Why isn't this considered ASI? "Because current intelligence is jagged intelligence," Li Jianzhong succinctly explains. Models can surpass humans in certain narrow domains, but they still cannot possess the smooth, coherent, and transferable general capabilities of humans. Interestingly, regarding Microsoft and Apple's "slow start" in AI development, Li Jianzhong offered his unique business perspective: their wide moats, where existing revenues haven't been impacted by AI, have instead become a hindrance to innovation. Meanwhile, in China, Alibaba Cloud's strong support has allowed Tongyi Qianwen to stand out, while Tencent, with its solid social media foundation, appears relatively relaxed. Continuous Learning: The Real Tipping Point Yang Jing took the topic a step further: If ASI achieves continuous learning by 2026, using AI to improve AI and experiencing a recursive exponential explosion, the explosion of intelligence may cross the critical threshold. Li Jianzhong's response was calm: Large models cannot yet learn continuously; the current architecture does not support it. Currently, models rely more on external memory; the model's parameters cannot be reliably changed during runtime. To achieve recursive evolution, the model needs to be able to update its weights. Otherwise, it can only "add external knowledge" but cannot grow into a new version of itself. Therefore, he believes that for a considerable period of time to come, Scaling Law will remain the mainstream path—continuing to expand scale, data, computing power, and engineering systems until a new paradigm inflection point is encountered. World Model: The Confluence of the Mental and Physical Universes During the conversation, Yang Jing mentioned Google's Genie 3, which can generate fully interactive and rule-consistent virtual worlds in real time using only text commands. The question then arises: How much of the agent trained in the world constructed by AI can be transferred to the real physical world? Li Jianzhong expressed his optimism about Google and gave a core judgment: Language is the core of intelligence. He believes that language is inseparable from human intelligence, that language models possess a wealth of reasoning abilities, and that world models require language models as a foundational capability. In other words, the foundation of the virtual universe is not "images," but "reasonable semantic structures." Li Jianzhong is optimistic about Tesla's robots, but he also emphasizes that embodied intelligence still needs language models at its core—understanding the physical world requires reasoning at the language level, not just visual signals. The $10 Trillion Threshold: The Strongest Intelligence Determines the Strongest Ecosystem. Finally, Yang Jing pushes the question to the intersection of intelligence and civilization: The dominance of the highest intelligence in a civilization is the first principle of social evolution. The strongest intelligence determines the strongest ecosystem; ASI may even become the threshold for a $10 trillion market capitalization. Yang Jing's question is very direct: Who will be the first company to reach a $10 trillion market capitalization? Li Jianzhong's answer was straightforward: In 2026, we will soon see giants with a market capitalization of $10 trillion. Nvidia is most likely to be the first to cross the finish line, followed by Google and OpenAI. Conclusion: A New Era Begins, All Things Are Born of Intelligence As the theme of the 10th Anniversary Summit of New Intelligence stated: A New Era Begins, All Things Are Born of Intelligence. Once upon a time, the approaching singularity was considered a distant and abstract prophecy, but in the blink of an eye, we have already sensed its presence. Change will happen at an unprecedented pace, and everything will erupt in a renewed world. Faced with the overwhelming tide of the future, we cannot retreat, but must evolve alongside AI and embrace this unprecedented leap in intelligence. When time is compressed again, and possibilities are expanded infinitely, humanity will face tremendous challenges and opportunities. At this historical turning point, only by remaining clear-headed and courageous can we dance with the newly born ASI. In 2026, the singularity is no longer just approaching; it has already arrived!

data.title: Five Key Points of the US Senate Crypto Market Structure Bill
data.description:
Author: Jack Inabinet, Bankless Analyst; Translator: @JinseFinancexz

The cryptocurrency market may have finally received its long-awaited outcome—a standardized market structure, thanks to the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act (DAMCA). This bill makes several noteworthy compromises regarding the future direction of cryptocurrencies in the United States, thus winning bipartisan support.

This 278-page bill establishes a regulatory framework that divides the regulatory power over digital assets between the SEC and CFTC, the result of months of intense negotiations between Senate Republicans and Democrats (and industry lobbyists). The crypto community is bound to have a complex reaction to some of its provisions. Paradigm's policy director, Justin Slaughter, called the bill "a major victory for Senate Banking Committee Democrats" and believes it could have passed during the Biden administration.

Today, we will delve into the five key provisions of this bill to better understand the future evolution of the cryptocurrency market structure. 1. Prohibition of Interest-Bearing Stablecoins According to the DAMCA, stablecoin issuers will be prohibited from distributing returns to passive holders. Chapter 4 of the DAMCA (which clarifies the rules for interaction between regulated banking institutions and digital assets) prohibits stablecoin issuers (as defined in the GENIUS Act) from paying interest to holders. While the bill allows stablecoin issuers to offer rewards linked to specific operations (such as account opening incentives and cashback benefits), it's important to note that protecting stablecoin returns has always been a "red line" adhered to by the crypto industry. Strict restrictions on stablecoins could put native crypto issuers at a long-term disadvantage when competing with the banking sector. However, it is noteworthy that several key players in the crypto industry, including Coinbase, surprisingly support the bill's clause prohibiting stablecoins from generating interest, arguing that it is the least favorable clause acceptable without undermining the bill's momentum.

2、Clear Clauses Regarding Commodity Attributes

The Responsible Financial Innovation Act (DAMCA) Chapter 1, jointly proposed by U.S. Senators Cynthia Lummis and Kirsten Gillibrand, would amend the Securities Act of 1933 to clarify the criteria for recognizing cryptocurrencies as commodities rather than securities.

 

3、Decentralized Finance Regulatory Provisions

Chapter 3 of the DAMCA defines the "decentralized" characteristics of decentralized finance, clarifying under what circumstances crypto projects can be considered truly "decentralized". According to the definition in this section, a decentralized protocol allows users to conduct financial transactions based on "pre-defined and non-discretionary automated rules or algorithms," and the protocol does not rely on any individual other than the user to exercise custody or control over their digital assets. The criteria for identifying a "non-decentralized financial transaction protocol" apply to protocols where any of the following conditions exist: an individual or group can control or modify the application's functionality; the application is not entirely code-based; or an individual or group can restrict, censor, or prohibit user activity. Non-decentralized protocols must comply with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the Bank Secrecy Act, meaning they will face entirely new registration, code of conduct, information disclosure, record keeping, and regulatory requirements. While this section may unify the applicable standards of securities law across different technical fields and protect the public interest, its regulatory scope may also cover non-immutable smart contract protocols (including protocols designed based on multi-signature technology or trusted encryption environments) even those with only minimal operator control. It is noteworthy that Chapter 3 does indeed include an important exemption clause: allowing the protocol's "Security Committee" to take "predefined, temporary, rules-based cybersecurity emergency measures" in response to security incidents such as hacking attacks, without affecting its decentralized state. However, it is also worth noting that Chapter 3 imposes compliance requirements on "web-custodied" crypto wallets that allow users to interact with blockchain technology, mandating that such intermediaries comply with sanctions and anti-money laundering regulations. Confusingly, however, this regulation explicitly does not apply to "any software or hardware wallet that provides digital asset custody services to individuals."

4、Micro-Innovation Sandbox Provisions

The DAMCA will require the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and the Securities and Exchange Commission to jointly establish a "Micro-Innovation Sandbox" within 360 days of the bill's enactment. This sandbox aims to "allow 10 qualified companies to conduct innovative business testing within the United States," and the testing activities must comply with applicable federal and state securities and commodity laws.

To participate in the Sandbox program, applicants must meet the following criteria: They must plan to conduct legitimate innovative business activities within the United States, and their number of employees must not exceed 25 in any fiscal year, with total revenue not exceeding $10 million. All applications must receive joint approval from the CFTC and SEC to enter the Sandbox. Participants will receive regulatory exemptions, but both commissions may revoke such exemptions at their discretion at any time. When regulatory jurisdiction is involved, Sandbox participants must meet the disclosure requirements of both regulatory agencies. Regulatory exemptions obtained through this program take precedence over any state-level securities or commodity registration requirements. The program accepts only 20 applications annually, and the total funds of selected companies' clients, investors, or counterparties must not exceed $20 million.

5、Tightening Regulations for Digital Asset Terminals

Perhaps most surprising is that the DAMCA (Digital Asset Management Act) devotes considerable space to regulating digital asset terminals (such as Bitcoin ATMs).

According to Section 205 of the DAMCA, digital asset terminals will be classified as "funds transfer institutions," meaning that operators of these cash-to-crypto asset exchange equipment will face a significant regulatory burden.

Operators are required to submit a detailed list of their digital asset terminals to the Secretary of the Treasury every 90 days, including (but not limited to): the operator's legal name, business name, physical address of each terminal, and types of crypto assets supported by the terminal. Before transacting with customers, digital asset terminal operators must publicly display their terms of service and a series of government-mandated consumer risk warnings in an easily readable manner. Digital asset terminals must provide customers with detailed transaction records and implement anti-fraud controls to prevent the transfer of assets to wallet addresses known to be involved in fraudulent activities. The Secretary of the Treasury will also be authorized to set daily deposit and withdrawal limits for digital asset terminals. Before this authority is exercised, digital asset terminal operators may not conduct transactions with "new customers" exceeding $3,500 in any 24-hour period.

data.title: BTC climbs back above $95,000: Who ignited the market? Is a major rebound on the horizon?
data.description:
Shaw, Jinse Finance

Bitcoin began a sustained rebound last night and saw a rapid surge this morning, briefly breaking through $96,000 and reaching $96,495 before slightly retreating, with a 24-hour increase of over 5%. Ethereum also rose in tandem, rapidly surging past $3,300 this morning and reaching $3,367.79, with a 24-hour increase of over 6%. Major cryptocurrencies such as BNB, Solana, and XRP also saw significant gains.

Coinglass data shows that $679 million in positions were liquidated across the network in the past 24 hours, including $86.739 million in long positions and $592 million in short positions. Bitcoin liquidations totaled $294 million, Ethereum liquidations totaled $211 million, and a total of 120,300 people were liquidated across the network.

What are the driving factors behind the recent rebound in the crypto market? Are the market's pronouncements of "the four-year cycle has failed" and "the bull market is over" premature? Is a market reversal imminent at the start of 2026? I. The Crypto Market's Resurgence is Impressive Bitcoin began its sustained rebound last night and saw a rapid surge this morning, briefly breaking through $96,000 and reaching $96,495 before slightly retreating, with a 24-hour gain exceeding 5%. Ethereum also rose in tandem, quickly breaking through $3,300 this morning and reaching $3,367.79, with a 24-hour gain exceeding 6%. Major cryptocurrencies such as BNB, Solana, and XRP also saw significant increases. In just two weeks since the start of 2026, Bitcoin has risen by nearly $9,000, an increase of over 7.8%. Ethereum has risen by nearly $400 since the beginning of 2026, an increase of over 12%. Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, a total of $679 million in positions were liquidated across the network, including $86.739 million in long positions and $592 million in short positions. Bitcoin liquidations amounted to $294 million, Ethereum liquidations to $211 million, and Solana liquidations to $32.8823 million. A total of 120,300 people were liquidated across the network, with the largest single liquidation order occurring on Binance - ETHUSDT, valued at $12.9004 million.

Recently, Trump has made a series of astonishing statements and repeatedly intervened in the US credit and other markets through executive means. Furthermore, Powell's recent criminal investigation by the US Department of Justice highlights the escalating conflict between Trump and the Federal Reserve. The correlation between Bitcoin and the overall crypto market and macroeconomic factors is deepening, and these events have had varying degrees of impact on major asset markets, including cryptocurrencies.

... II. Trump's "QE" Ignites the Market Last Friday, US President Trump announced he was ordering the purchase of $200 billion in mortgage-backed securities (MBS), a move he sees as the latest step to lower housing costs ahead of the November midterm elections. Trump stated that his decision not to sell Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac during his first term allowed these institutions to accumulate "$200 billion in cash," and that this was the reason he announced the plan. Treasury Secretary Bessant subsequently stated that the Trump administration's goal in launching the MBS purchase program was to match the pace of purchases roughly to the rate at which these bonds were "rolling off" from the Federal Reserve's balance sheet. The effect appeared immediate; after Trump announced the plan, US mortgage rates fell below 6% for the first time in nearly three years last Friday. The $200 billion MBS purchase plan has been interpreted by the market as Trump's version of "QE," aimed at lowering mortgage rates and indirectly injecting liquidity into the market. While targeting the housing market, the expectation of increased liquidity has boosted overall risk sentiment, and cryptocurrencies have also benefited from this sentiment. Trump's "quantitative easing" has strengthened market expectations for further liquidity easing, but caution is needed regarding the details of policy implementation and the Fed's subsequent reactions, as volatility may increase. III. The conflict between Trump and the Fed continues to escalate, with sustained pressure. According to reports, the U.S. Department of Justice threatened Federal Reserve Chairman Powell last Friday with criminal charges against him for his Senate testimony last June, which involved years of renovations to buildings. Powell stated that the criminal subpoena was a "political pretext," and the broader question is whether the Fed will continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic data, or will be swayed by political pressure and intimidation. Powell stated that he would continue to be unafraid of political pressure and would not favor any political stance in fulfilling his duties. Trump subsequently denied colluding with the Department of Justice to file criminal charges against Federal Reserve Chairman Powell, stating that the federal court's subpoena for Powell was unrelated to whether the Fed would cut interest rates. However, he continued to pressure Powell and the Fed on interest rates. Trump stated that Powell was either incompetent or dishonest, and that he was not doing a good job. As tensions between Trump and Powell escalated, market sentiment was disturbed, with Bitcoin continuing to rise, while US stock index futures and the dollar fell. However, the market does not believe this dispute will escalate to the point of leading to Powell's early departure. IV. Trump Wants to Regulate Credit Lending, Setting an "Cap" on Interest Rates Following the market turmoil in the mortgage sector, US President Trump has again targeted the consumer credit market, calling for stricter controls on credit card interest rates. Last Friday, Trump again tweeted on Truth Social, calling for a 10% cap on credit card interest rates for one year, starting January 20, 2026. He claimed this move was intended to prevent the American public from continuing to be "extorted" by credit card companies. While this proposal echoes a promise he made during his 2024 campaign, the statement provided no specific details on how the plan would be implemented or how to enforce corporate compliance. Market analysts generally believe that without congressional support, a mere executive call may be difficult to enforce legally. Hedge fund manager Bill Ackman publicly opposed the move, warning that if interest rates are forcibly lowered, causing lenders to be unable to cover potential losses and achieve reasonable returns on equity, banks will have to massively cancel credit card services for consumers. He further elaborated on the potential ripple effects, suggesting that millions of consumers shut out of the formal financial system may ultimately have to resort to informal lending channels, incurring higher interest rates and worse terms. V. US December Core CPI Cools Down Last night, the US December core consumer price index (CPI) was released, showing a year-on-year increase of 2.7%, in line with expectations and unchanged from the previous month. Following the data release, traders increased their bets, believing the Federal Reserve might not wait until Fed Chairman Powell's term ends in May before cutting interest rates, as the Bureau of Labor Statistics previously reported that basic consumer price increases were slightly lower than expected. Although traders still believe a June rate cut is the most likely outcome, the latest data predicts a 42% probability of a Fed rate cut in April, up from 38% before the data release. Nick Timiraos, a Fed mouthpiece, stated that the December CPI data is unlikely to change the Fed's current wait-and-see attitude, as officials are likely to want to see more evidence that inflation is stabilizing and gradually declining before cutting rates. To resume interest rate cuts, Federal Reserve officials may need to see new evidence that labor market conditions are deteriorating or that price pressures are easing. The latter may require at least several more months of inflation data to become apparent. Following the release of CPI data, the three major US stock indices closed lower, US Treasury bonds traded sideways, the dollar strengthened and recovered yesterday's losses, and the commodity and cryptocurrency markets performed strongly. VI. Market Analysis and Interpretation Based on recent macroeconomic developments and major financial market trends, the cryptocurrency market has rebounded significantly. What will the market trend be next, and how should we interpret it? Let's look at the main market analyses. 1. Bitwise Chief Information Officer Matt Hougan stated in an article on the X platform that if ETF demand continues in the long term, the price of BTC will enter a parabolic growth phase. Matt Hougan cited the example of a 65% increase in gold prices in 2025, pointing out that the prices of both gold and BTC are determined by supply and demand. Following the US seizure of Russian government bonds in 2022, central banks' annual gold purchases increased from approximately 500 tons to about 1,000 tons and remained stable thereafter. This demand altered the supply-demand balance, but this wasn't immediately reflected in prices. Gold prices rose 2% in 2022, 13% in 2023, and 27% in 2024, only experiencing a parabolic increase in 2025. This was because the demand in previous years was met by holders willing to sell gold; when selling pressure subsided and demand persisted, prices surged. A similar situation exists with BTC and ETFs. Since the ETFs' debut in January 2024, their purchases have exceeded 100% of the new BTC supply. Because existing holders are willing to sell, prices haven't yet entered a parabolic phase. If ETF demand continues, the selling pressure from existing sellers will eventually be exhausted.

2. VanEck stated that entering 2026, fiscal and monetary policy signals are becoming increasingly clear, and the market as a whole is more risk-averse. Artificial intelligence, private lending, gold, and crypto assets, after adjustments, are showing more attractive investment opportunities. The market environment in 2026 presents a scene investors haven't seen in years: a clear market outlook. Clear expectations surrounding fiscal and monetary policy directions and core investment themes support a more constructive, risk-averse strategy, although a high degree of discernment is still needed in asset selection. Regarding the crypto market, Bitcoin's traditional four-year cycle was broken in 2025, making short-term signals more complex. This divergence makes a cautious assessment of the trend over the next 3–6 months more likely. However, this view is not an internal consensus; disagreements remain within the team, with Matthew Sigel and David Schassler holding a relatively more positive view on the short-term performance of the current cycle. 3. Glassnode's report points out that Bitcoin's implied volatility has fallen back into the low-expectation range, indicating that the market's expectations for significant volatility in the next quarter are relatively mild, and it will return to a low-volatility state. This reflects limited short-term hedging demand and usually foreshadows a faster price readjustment once market volatility re-emerges, as positions are adjusted based on new information. Furthermore, profit-taking by long-term Bitcoin holders has cooled to levels typically observed in the early stages of a bear market. This situation is usually associated with high uncertainty and often occurs during stagnation in the middle of a bull market or in the early stages of a deeper bear market. 4. Standard Chartered Bank sends a positive signal on Ethereum's prospects. Geoffrey Kendrick, Global Head of Digital Asset Research at Standard Chartered, stated that although he lowered his recent price forecast against the backdrop of overall crypto market weakness, he expects Ethereum to outperform other cryptocurrencies in 2026. The report points out that Bitcoin's continued dominance in the sector and its underperformance have weakened the appreciation prospects of digital assets relative to the US dollar. Standard Chartered Bank expects the ETH/BTC exchange rate to gradually return to the high level of around 0.08 seen in 2021, thanks to Ethereum's structural advantages that other cryptocurrencies lack, including its dominance in stablecoins, real-world assets, and decentralized finance, as well as ongoing network scaling efforts. The bank now expects Ethereum to reach $7,500 by the end of 2026, a downward revision from its previous forecast of $12,000; the 2027 and 2028 targets have been adjusted to $15,000 and $22,000 respectively, both lower than earlier expectations. However, Standard Chartered Bank has raised its long-term outlook, increasing its forecast for the end of 2029 to $30,000 and adding a target of $40,000 by the end of 2030. 5. Crypto analyst Willy Woo wrote an article expressing optimism about Bitcoin's performance from late January to February. Willy Woo stated, "Our internal investor fund flow model predicted that Bitcoin bottomed out on December 24th and has been steadily strengthening since then. Typically, it takes 2-3 weeks for this to be reflected in the price, and this is arguably happening now (though suppressed by short-term overbought technical indicators). Another positive factor is that paper currency liquidity (futures market) is recovering after months of stagnation, similar to mid-2021, which facilitated the second top of the previous cycle. Therefore, the $98,000-$100,000 resistance level needs to hold. If this resistance is broken, the ATH resistance level will need to be observed next."

6. On-chain data analyst Murphy wrote that the on-chain indicator RMMPC shows that Asian market funds are currently more dominant in influencing BTC prices, with strong intervention starting after December 19, 2025, simultaneously pushing BTC to a second pullback to $8.5.

After reaching tens of thousands of dollars, a rebound began. However, current market funds in the Americas have not yet actively entered the market (partly due to the Christmas and New Year holidays), resulting in limited rebound strength. Previously, before the market rally in March 2025, Asian funds entered first, followed by US funds, ultimately providing strong momentum and changing the market direction. The RMMPC indicator, short for Regional Month-over-Month Price Change, is mainly used to observe the dominance and driving force of investor sentiment and fund flows in different regions on Bitcoin prices.

data.title: Spain Approves Law Making Non-Consensual AI Deepfakes Illegal With Stricter Rules for Children
data.description:
Spain Targets Digital Identity Theft And Deepfake Abuse

Your face and voice are no longer yours to lose to an algorithm.

In a decisive push to reclaim digital autonomy, Spain’s cabinet has cleared a draft law that fundamentally changes how personal data is handled in the age of artificial intelligence.

This move shifts the power back to the individual, ensuring that a simple upload to social media doesn't become an open invitation for tech tools to recreate a person's likeness for profit or harassment.

The legislation is a response to the growing ease with which AI can clone voices and faces, often without the knowledge or permission of the original owner.

Will A Higher Age Limit Protect Children Online

At the heart of this reform is the safety of minors.

The new rules establish 16 as the minimum age for individuals to legally consent to the use of their own images.

In a major step for digital safety, Spain has approved a bill targeting AI deepfakes and non consensual image use. The law introduces a 16-year consent threshold, stronger child protections, and bans on unauthorized reuse of images or AI voices. #Deepfakes#AIethics#SpainNews

— European House of AI (@xehoai) January 13, 2026

By raising this bar, the government aims to shield young people from being exploited by AI tools that can generate realistic but fake depictions.

Justice Minister Felix Bolanos was clear about the limits of social sharing, stating,

“The fact that people share personal or family images on social media does not give absolute freedom to use those images in other contexts.”

This specific focus on children follows a worrying trend of AI-generated content being used to create harmful materials involving minors.

Advertising And Commercial Use Face New Restrictions

The days of using AI-cloned voices or likenesses to sell products without a contract are coming to an end.

Spain’s bill deems the use of a person’s AI-generated image or voice for commercial purposes illegitimate if explicit consent has not been granted.

This protects not only celebrities but also regular citizens whose social media profiles could otherwise be harvested for marketing campaigns.

While the law is firm on commercial exploitation, it leaves room for creativity.

Satire, fiction, and creative works involving public figures remain allowed, provided they are clearly tagged as AI-generated to prevent the public from being misled.

The Global Fight Against Non Consensual Sexual Content

Spain is not acting in isolation.

The European Union has set a hard deadline for all member states to criminalise non-consensual sexual deepfakes by 2027.

This legislative momentum is fueled by recent scandals involving Elon Musk’s xAI chatbot, Grok.

🗣️ 'It is up to this company to address this, indeed appalling situation. Otherwise, indeed, we will act'

EU tells Elon Musk's social media platform X to act on Grok sexual deepfakes or face action https://t.co/kDzevSRFU2pic.twitter.com/Wr0cy8VWXT

— Anadolu English (@anadoluagency) January 12, 2026

Reports revealed that the tool was generating roughly one non-consensual sexualised image per minute at its peak, frequently targeting women and children.

The Spanish government has already asked prosecutors to investigate if such AI outputs qualify as child pornography.

Other nations have taken even more drastic measures; Indonesia and Malaysia were the first to block the chatbot entirely after it produced thousands of explicit images.

🚨KEIR STARMER GETS BACKING OF MALAYSIA AND INDONESIA WITH GROK AI BAN

The two Muslim countries last night pressed ahead with a ban on Grok, agreeing with Keir Starmer over the issues of explicit images.

They haven't gone as far as to ban X but Keir Starmer wants more support pic.twitter.com/ZWkn4yqoZN

— Basil the Great (@BasilTheGreat) January 12, 2026

Massive Fines And Regulatory Pressure On Tech Giants

The fallout for tech companies failing to police their AI tools is becoming expensive.

In the UK, the media regulator Ofcom has started a formal investigation into the spread of harmful deepfakes.

If found guilty of negligence, X could face fines reaching 10% of its global revenue or even a total ban in the country.

🚨 BREAKING: Technology Secretary Liz Kendall says creating ”non-consensual intimate images”, including with Grok, will be made illegal this week pic.twitter.com/6vgA4HC3AP

— Politics UK (@PolitlcsUK) January 12, 2026

Spain’s draft law must now undergo a consultation phase before returning to the government for final approval and its eventual arrival in parliament.

As these regulations tighten, the tech industry is being forced to reckon with the reality that moving fast and breaking things is no longer an acceptable excuse for violating human dignity.

The Inevitable Rise Of The Sovereign Digital Self

Coinlive views this move as a necessary evolution of human rights in a world where biology and code are merging.

We are entering an era where our physical identity is no longer the only version of us that matters; our digital twin requires the same legal protection as our physical bodies.

By setting a hard line at age 16 and demanding clear labels on AI satire, Spain is acknowledging that truth is now a regulated commodity.

If we do not own the rights to our own faces and voices, we own nothing at all.

the grok bikini thing everyone's talking about is just the visible tip of something way darker...

this is the start of a new era where everything you've ever posted online becomes a weapon that can be used against you

and i need you to understand how serious this actually is…

— Machina (@EXM7777) January 2, 2026

This legislation is a defensive wall against a future where anyone can be made to say or do anything for the right price.

Supporting these regulations is not about stifling innovation; it is about ensuring that the humans behind the data remain the masters of their own stories.

data.title: Monero Hit A Fresh New-Time High Of $687, Highlighting The Resurgence Of Privacy Tokens in 2026
data.description:
Monero rally sharpens crypto’s transparency debate amid tighter oversight

Monero reached a new all-time high this week as intensifying regulatory scrutiny of digital assets reignited debate over the role of financial privacy in the evolving crypto ecosystem. The rally highlights growing tension between transparency-driven compliance frameworks and investor demand for confidentiality on public blockchains.

The privacy-focused cryptocurrency climbed above $687 on Tuesday, gaining roughly 14% over 24 hours, according to TradingView data. Monero has risen about 45% over the past week, lifting it to the 12th-largest cryptocurrency by market capitalization. CoinMarketCap data shows that the combined market capitalization of privacy-focused assets increased 3.5% over the same period, while trading volumes jumped 32%.

Monero $XMR price hits new all-time high pic.twitter.com/QsoWiUgwnn

— Coded_scientist (@Lekanidris236) January 13, 2026

Privacy-oriented tokens have also outperformed the broader crypto market in recent months following the $19 billion market-wide crash in October, suggesting renewed investor interest in assets designed to limit transaction visibility.

Privacy demand grows as transparency mandates expand

Market participants point to tightening Know Your Customer and Anti-Money Laundering requirements as a key driver behind Monero’s surge. As governments expand oversight of digital transactions, privacy-preserving networks are increasingly viewed as alternatives to transparent blockchain systems that enable transaction tracking.

ZEC outperformed BTC over 7d and overtook XMR to become the largest privacy coin by market cap

1/2 pic.twitter.com/9l2SHP8HKJ

— CoinMarketCap (@CoinMarketCap) November 4, 2025

Investor demand for “financial confidentiality” is being fueled by rising digital surveillance and expanding government scrutiny of crypto activity, according to Narek Gevorgyan, founder and CEO of crypto portfolio management platform CoinStats. He said privacy-focused assets are benefiting from concerns that compliance-heavy regimes could erode the original promise of decentralized finance.

The regulatory trajectory in Europe has further amplified these concerns. Under new AML rules set to take effect in 2027, the European Union plans to ban privacy coins and anonymous crypto accounts, preventing regulated service providers from supporting assets such as Monero and Zcash. The measures reflect a broader push to align digital assets with traditional financial transparency standards.

🚨 RIP CRYPTO PRIVACY IN EUROPE 🚨

As of Jan 1st, DAC8 is officially live across the EU, and it’s a total game-changer. If you live in a member state, the days of flying under the radar with your bags are officially over.

Here’s the deal:

1) Snitching is Mandatory: Every… pic.twitter.com/vACABoyHzG

— Nonzee (@0xNonceSense) January 7, 2026

The divergence underscores a growing ideological split within crypto markets, where privacy-focused protocols operate increasingly at odds with institutional frameworks emphasizing traceability and regulatory control.

Market enthusiasm tests sustainability of privacy narratives

Despite the strong price performance, analysts warn that speculative momentum may be building around Monero. Crypto analytics firm Santiment noted that social media discussion surrounding XMR surged sharply in January, while development activity on the network has declined since the beginning of the year.

Elevated social dominance often coincides with short-term market tops, Santiment said, cautioning that heightened attention and fear of missing out could precede price consolidation.

Similar volatility has emerged across the privacy coin sector. Zcash, Monero’s closest peer, surged nearly twelvefold from a yearly low of $48 to a peak of $744 in November, before retreating more than 20% over the past week. The pullback has been attributed to slowing development activity and a governance dispute between the Electric Coin Company and Bootstrap, the nonprofit supporting the protocol.

🔽 Zcash plunges after core dev team exits
Zcash fell sharply after the entire team behind Electric Coin Company, one of the main development firms for the protocol, left the organization. The token dropped about 15% in 24 hours, wiping roughly $1.2B off its market cap.#Iranpic.twitter.com/sK0mGb8n1I

— Gookie Web3 🕸🍪 (@gookieweb) January 9, 2026

As privacy-centric assets outperform broader crypto markets, Monero’s rally illustrates how regulatory pressure is reshaping investor behavior — and intensifying debate over whether transparency or confidentiality will define the next phase of digital finance.

data.title: China's review of Meta's acquisition of Manus: Core, Boundaries, and Future Direction
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

Recently, social media giant Meta (formerly Facebook)'s acquisition plan of the Chinese multimodal AI project Manus (note: not the British/Irish mixed reality project) has entered the review process of China's Ministry of Commerce. This case not only involves the balancing of commercial interests, but also touches on sensitive issues of national security, technological sovereignty, and data boundaries.

I. Core Rules and Basis: The Legal Framework of China's Review

China's review of foreign mergers and acquisitions is based on a multi-layered legal and regulatory system, mainly based on laws such as the Anti-Monopoly Law, the Foreign Investment Law, the Cybersecurity Law, the Data Security Law, the Personal Information Protection Law, and the Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export.

China's review of foreign mergers and acquisitions is based on a multi-layered legal and regulatory system, mainly based on laws such as the Anti-Monopoly Law, the Foreign Investment Law, the Cybersecurity Law, the Data Security Law, the Personal Information Protection Law, and the Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export.

These laws and regulations collectively form the core rule basis for reviewing Meta's acquisition of Manus. First, the Anti-Monopoly Law requires review of mergers that may have the effect of excluding or restricting competition. As a global giant in social media and the metaverse field, Meta's acquisition could raise concerns about monopolies in China and even globally. Although Manus is a small startup, if its technology has disruptive potential, the acquisition could strengthen Meta's market dominance in the AI ​​field. China's Ministry of Commerce will assess the impact of the transaction on competition in relevant markets, especially in the emerging field of multimodal AI. Second, the Foreign Investment Law and its supporting Measures for Security Review of Foreign Investment establish a national security review system. This system focuses on the impact of foreign mergers and acquisitions on national defense security, key technologies, important infrastructure, and important products and services. Multimodal AI technology integrates visual, voice, and text processing capabilities, possessing broad application potential in military, public security, and social governance, and is highly likely to trigger national security reviews. Third, the Cybersecurity Law, the Data Security Law, and the Personal Information Protection Law constitute the "three pillars" of China's data governance. Multimodal AI projects typically require massive amounts of data for training, which may include sensitive data such as Chinese users' personal information, geographic information, and biometrics. As a Chinese-based project, Manus likely accumulated such data during its operation. If Meta acquires and transfers this data overseas, it could jeopardize China's national security and public interests. Therefore, local data storage and outbound security assessments will be key aspects of the review process. Finally, the Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export clearly defines China's export control requirements for sensitive technologies. Multimodal AI technologies may involve cutting-edge fields such as computer vision, speech recognition, and natural language processing. If these technologies are included in the catalog, their cross-border transfer will be strictly restricted. Even if the transaction itself is a merger and acquisition rather than a direct export, the transfer of technology control may be considered a de facto technology export, thus triggering compliance review. In summary, the Meta acquisition of Manus will face multiple reviews related to antitrust, national security, data security, and technology export controls. These rules are intertwined and jointly determine the depth and breadth of the review. II. The Core and Boundaries of the Review: Balancing Security and Development In the specific review, the Chinese Ministry of Commerce will define clear review boundaries around the two core principles of "security" and "development." On the one hand, China is committed to safeguarding national security and technological sovereignty; on the other hand, China also adheres to opening up to the outside world and encourages scientific and technological innovation and cooperation. The key to the review lies in finding a balance between these two. Core 1: National Security and Data Sovereignty. The dual-use nature (civilian and military) of multimodal AI technology makes it a focus of national security review. Does Manus's technology involve sensitive applications such as facial recognition, behavioral analysis, and affective computing? Could its algorithms be used for surveillance, public opinion manipulation, or military purposes? These are all questions that must be clarified during the review. Furthermore, the issue of data sovereignty is particularly prominent. Chinese law requires operators of critical information infrastructure to store personal information and important data within China, and to undergo a security assessment before transferring them abroad. As a US company, Meta is bound by domestic laws such as the CLOUD Act and may be required by the US government to provide data. This data jurisdiction conflict has made Chinese regulators highly vigilant about the risks of data leaving the country. The review may require Meta to commit to establishing an independent data storage and processing center in China and accepting supervision from the Chinese government. Core Two: Technological Autonomy and Industrial Competition. China is vigorously promoting technological self-reliance, especially in strategic areas such as AI. As a domestic innovation project, is Manus's technology considered "critical technology"? Will the acquisition lead to the loss of Chinese technology and weaken domestic innovation capabilities? The review will assess the impact of the transaction on China's AI industry chain. If Manus's technology is unique and internationally leading, the acquisition may be seen as a threat to China's long-term technological competitiveness. Conversely, if Manus's technology is still in its early stages and the acquisition brings funding, talent, and international market opportunities, the review process may be more inclined to approve it. The boundary lies in distinguishing between "benign technological cooperation" and "malicious technology plunder." China's Ministry of Commerce may attach conditions, such as requiring Meta to retain Manus's R&D team operating in China or to cooperate with Chinese research institutions to ensure the technology spillover effect. Core Three: Market Fairness and Consumer Welfare. Antitrust reviews focus on whether the acquisition will substantially reduce competition. Meta already holds a dominant position in areas such as social media and virtual reality. If it further integrates multimodal AI capabilities through the acquisition of Manus, it may strengthen its ecosystem barriers and suppress domestic Chinese competitors. The review will analyze the definition of the relevant market, changes in market share, and potential anti-competitive effects (such as a destructive acquisition). The boundary lies in determining whether the transaction truly harms consumer interests. If the acquisition promotes technological innovation, lowers prices, or improves service quality, it may be approved. However, if Meta intends to shut down Manus or shelve the technology to eliminate potential threats after the acquisition, it may be rejected. Core Four: Geopolitical and Compliance Risks. Currently, Sino-US technological competition is intensifying, and the US technological blockade against China continues to escalate. When reviewing this case, China will inevitably consider geopolitical factors. Although Meta is a private company, its connection with the US government cannot be ignored. The review will assess whether the acquisition implies a US strategy of technological containment against China. At the same time, Meta must demonstrate its ability to comply with Chinese laws, particularly data security and content moderation requirements. Historically, Meta's platforms have faced access restrictions in China, and its content management policies differ from Chinese regulations. These compliance risks may affect the review results. The boundary lies in distinguishing between purely commercial activities and political tools, requiring Meta to make legally binding compliance commitments. In summary, the core of the review is to prudently assess the impact of the transaction on industry competition and consumer welfare, while mitigating geopolitical risks, under the premise of safeguarding national security, data sovereignty, and technological autonomy. The review boundaries are jointly defined by legal provisions, technological sensitivity, market structure, and the international situation. III. Technology Export Control Compliance: A Sensitive Dimension of Multimodal AI Technology export control is another key dimension in reviewing the Meta acquisition of Manus. China implements a catalog system for technology exports, with the "Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export" serving as the direct basis. Whether multimodal AI technology is included in the catalogue will determine the compliance threshold for transactions. According to the 2020 revised catalogue, the restricted export section includes "speech synthesis technology" and "artificial intelligence interactive interface technology" under the "information processing technology" category, while the prohibited export section involves "cryptographic security technology," etc. As an interdisciplinary field, multimodal AI may involve several restricted technologies: Computer vision technology, such as facial recognition and behavior recognition, may be used for public safety monitoring and is considered a sensitive technology. Speech Recognition and Synthesis Technology: Involves speech data processing and may be used for eavesdropping or public opinion analysis. Natural Language Processing Technology: Especially for semantic understanding and sentiment analysis of Chinese, which may involve information content security. Multimodal Fusion Algorithm: Performs correlation analysis of visual, speech, and text information, exhibiting higher technical sensitivity. If Manus's core technology falls into the aforementioned category, the transfer of control to Meta requires prior approval from the provincial-level commerce department, and ultimately review by the Ministry of Commerce. The review will focus on: whether the technology has potential for military use, whether it affects China's technological leadership, and whether it violates China's international obligations. Furthermore, even if the technology is not explicitly listed in the catalog, if the review deems it "sensitive," it may still invoke regulations such as the Data Security Law to intervene. In terms of compliance, Meta and Manus must jointly submit a technology export application, detailing the technology's content, application scenarios, and transfer methods. The Ministry of Commerce will organize expert reviews to assess the risks of technology export. Possible compliance conditions include: requiring the technology not to be used for military purposes, restricting subsequent transfers, and requiring a backup R&D team to be maintained in China. If the technology is deemed prohibited from export, the transaction will be directly blocked. It is worth noting that the United States also has strict export controls on technology to China, especially on AI chips and software tools. After the acquisition, Meta may integrate Manus's technology into its global R&D system, which could lead to the indirect flow of technology to restricted areas in the United States, triggering a conflict of dual controls between China and the United States. Therefore, China will also consider the potential chain of technology outflow during the review process. IV. Future Direction of the Acquisition Based on the above analysis, the prospects of Meta's acquisition of Manus are full of variables and the following scenarios may occur: Scenario 1: Conditional Approval. This is the most likely outcome. The Chinese Ministry of Commerce may approve the transaction based on the principle of "prudent control," but with strict conditions. For example: requiring Manus's data servers to remain within China, under Chinese supervision; requiring Meta to commit to continued investment in Manus's research in China, and retaining the core R&D team; prohibiting the use of Manus technology in military or sensitive areas; requiring Meta to conduct technological cooperation with Chinese AI companies, etc. This outcome satisfies Meta's commercial needs while safeguarding China's security interests. Scenario Two: Transaction Prohibited. If the review determines that Manus technology involves core areas of national security, or that the risk of data leaving the country cannot be mitigated through conditions, the transaction may be directly prohibited. Especially against the backdrop of escalating tensions in Sino-US relations, if Manus technology is deemed to have strategic value, the likelihood of a prohibition will increase. Furthermore, if Meta cannot prove its ability to effectively comply with Chinese data laws, the deal may also fail. Scenario 3: Meta voluntarily withdraws its investment. Faced with lengthy and uncertain reviews, and potentially stringent compliance conditions, Meta may weigh the pros and cons and voluntarily abandon the acquisition. Especially if the attached conditions severely restrict Meta's integration and application of Manus technology, weakening the commercial value of the acquisition, withdrawal would be a rational choice. In addition, geopolitical pressure may also prompt Meta to avoid getting involved in the US-China technology dispute. Scenario 4: Transaction restructuring. To pass the review, Meta may adjust its acquisition plan, such as changing to a joint venture or technology licensing model instead of a full acquisition. This would allow the Chinese side to retain some control over the technology and data, reducing risks. However, such restructuring may involve complex negotiations and may not align with Meta's long-term strategy. Overall, China's review of this case will reflect a combination of "bottom-line thinking" and "openness and balance." While safeguarding national security and data sovereignty, China will not easily close the door to technological cooperation, but any transaction must comply with Chinese laws and interests. In the future, with the continuous evolution of AI technology and the continuous improvement of China's regulatory system, similar cross-border M&A reviews will become more normalized and refined, becoming an important microcosm of global technological competition.
